coredata.dc:title: Revealing the effect of initial conditions and the potential of self-heating versus active heating on fast-charged lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Abo Gamra, Kareem
Wassiliadis, Nikolaos
Allgäuer, Christian
Lienkamp, Markus
coredata.dc:description: Ultra-fast charging of battery electric vehicles (BEVs) with a charging time of under 15 minutes has been identified as a solution to increase long-range suitability and allow for a higher degree of vehicle utilization. At the same time, it poses a critical edge case for the safe and sustainable management of lithium-ion batteries. Initial vehicle conditions, such as state-of-charge (SoC) and battery temperature at the start of fast charging, are becoming increasingly relevant, as they predefine the charging capability of the vehicle. To evaluate this, we compared two battery cells with different self-heating behaviors using experimental tests and electrochemical battery models when utilizing an anode potential control strategy to prevent lithium plating. The results reveal that a starting SoC in a range of 20–50% at low temperatures can lead to an over tenfold increase in charging time depending on the utilized cell, as the batteries do not heat up sufficiently compared to a starting SoC at 0%, which becomes more critical as the battery ages. This emphasizes the need for improved cell designs and material compositions, as well as intelligent operation and thermal management strategies such as preheating, which is demonstrated to reduce charging time.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Battery electric vehicles
Lithium-ion battery
Fast charging
Lithium plating
Preconditioning
Thermal management

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid fundingbodyid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight oauserlicense openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder sponsoredaccesstype srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content oa subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Influence of initial conditions on charging duration

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: German Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Energy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the funding provided by the German Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Energy(BMWi) within the project “ultraBatt” under grant number 01MV21015D . The authors would like to thank Batemo for providing battery models and teardown data for the TerraE INR18650-25P cell, as well as the BMZ Group for providing the cells. The authors also thank Thomas Schöpfel for his support of this study during his master’s studies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Charge duration to 80% SoC at different starting SoCs for a Tesla Model 3 battery in an on average cool (17°C) and warm (38°C) initial state.

Tesla Model 3 fast-charge processes at a 250kW IONITY charger for a preconditioned battery when targeting a Tesla Supercharger within navigation compared to an unconditioned battery without prior heating. (a) and (b) show the battery and cooling inlet temperature as well as the SoC for the conditioned case, while (c) and (d) display the unconditioned case [36].

Voltage and temperature development for a Sony/Murata VTC5A and TerraE 25P cell charged from 1C to 6C at 0°C. Subfigures (a) and (b) show the VTC5A, while subfigures (c) and (d) show the TerraE 25P cell.

Example of the simulated charging procedure for the VTC5A cell with an initial charging rate of 6C from 0% to 80% SoC at a starting temperature of -10°C using an anode potential control strategy showing the charging current and the anode potential. The dotted line represents the anode potential threshold, including a safety margin of 50mV to prevent control undershoots because of inaccurate model-based anode potential predictions.

Sensitivity of the charging time until 80% SoC to variations in starting temperature and state of charge of the battery electric vehicle for the lithium-ion battery cells when utilizing an anode potential control strategy. (a)–(c) show the VTC5A and (d)–(f) the TerraE 25P with a 1C to 4C maximum current limit.

Charging time increase for a 4C charge process until 80% SoC due to variations in starting temperature and state of charge at (a) -10°C starting temperature and (b) 40% starting SoC, relative to the shortest respective charging time at hypothetical SoHs of 100%, 90%, and 80%.

TerraE 4C fast-charging durations with simultaneous heating for different starting and target temperatures with heating powers of 0.33W, 1W, and 15W and initial SoCs of 0% and 40%. The durations are shown for a new cell as well as an aged cell at 80% SoH.

VTC5A 4C fast-charging durations with simultaneous heating for different starting and target temperatures with heating powers of 0.33W, 1W, and 15W and initial SoCs of 0% and 40%. The durations are shown for a new cell as well as an aged cell at 80% SoH.
TerraE INR18650-25P geometrical design. The measurements are given in mm.
Sony/Murata US18650VTC5A geometrical design. The measurements are given in mm.
Overview of preheating methodologies reported in the literature, compiled from earlier studies [8–12].
Datasheet specifications of the lithium-ion batteries under study [40].

head.ce:title: Revealing the effect of initial conditions and the potential of self-heating versus active heating on fast-charged lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technical University of Munich (TUM), School of Engineering & Design, Department of Mobility Systems Engineering, Institute of Automotive Technology, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Technical University of Munich (TUM), School of Engineering & Design, Department of Mobility Systems Engineering, Institute of Automotive Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Technical University of Munich (TUM), School of Engineering & Design, Department of Mobility Systems Engineering, Institute of Automotive Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultra-fast charging of battery electric vehicles (BEVs) with a charging time of under 15 minutes has been identified as a solution to increase long-range suitability and allow for a higher degree of vehicle utilization. At the same time, it poses a critical edge case for the safe and sustainable management of lithium-ion batteries. Initial vehicle conditions, such as state-of-charge (SoC) and battery temperature at the start of fast charging, are becoming increasingly relevant, as they predefine the charging capability of the vehicle. To evaluate this, we compared two battery cells with different self-heating behaviors using experimental tests and electrochemical battery models when utilizing an anode potential control strategy to prevent lithium plating. The results reveal that a starting SoC in a range of 20–50% at low temperatures can lead to an over tenfold increase in charging time depending on the utilized cell, as the batteries do not heat up sufficiently compared to a starting SoC at 0%, which becomes more critical as the battery ages. This emphasizes the need for improved cell designs and material compositions, as well as intelligent operation and thermal management strategies such as preheating, which is demonstrated to reduce charging time.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Measurement and simulation data related to this article can be found via https://doi.org/10.14459/2024mp1735496, provided by mediaTUM.

ce:section.ce:para: For battery electric vehicles (BEVs) to become more viable as a long-term alternative to the combustion engine, hurdles such as cost, recycling, safety, and range anxiety must be addressed [1]. One part of the solution to these issues is the implementation of novel fast-charging strategies, which different institutions have positioned as a development focus for the future [2]. This is especially relevant for the commercial vehicle sector, which has so far experienced little electrification due to the inacceptability of long pause durations [2]. The United States Department of Energy (DOE) named a charge duration of under 15min by 2028 as a future goal [3], while the European Technology and Innovation Platform (ETIP) targets charging durations of under 20min by 2030 [4]. To achieve these goals, improvements to conventional battery systems have to be implemented to achieve higher charging speeds with reduced degradation, ranging from the material level to the vehicle packaging level. These include topics such as novel cell chemistries, manufacturing processes, operation limits, advanced battery and thermal management systems, novel battery pack welding and assembling techniques, or advanced operation strategies on vehicle level [1,2]. By utilizing battery models capable of depicting the internal states of the deployed lithium-ion cell, specific operation strategies can be developed to operate along the batteries physical boundaries, thus allowing an improved utilization while simultaneously limiting adverse effects on safety and lifetime [2]. For ultra-fast-charging strategies, model-based charge protocols can be developed to avoid the most dominant aging mechanisms that occur at high charge rates [2]. Previous literature has identified these to predominantly include lithium plating at high C-rates and low temperatures, during which lithium is deposited on the anode, as well as accelerated side reactions due to the resulting temperature increase during the fast-charge process, which includes mechanisms such as solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) growth [5,6]. Given the nature of these degradation mechanisms, the overall aging behavior and performance of lithium-ion batteries during fast charging have been observed to depend on the thermal environment as well as other initial conditions such as the state-of-charge (SoC) [7]. Contrary to previous findings, this has led recent literature to argue that higher temperatures can be beneficial for charge duration as well as aging mitigation due to the resulting improvements in transfer kinetics and reduced risk of lithium plating, which offsets the negative impact of the duration at a higher temperature while charging [6,7]. This has led a number of automotive manufacturers to include preconditioning strategies prior to fast-charge events to heat the battery to more beneficial temperatures. The optimal temperature for fast charging, however, depends not only on the initial conditions of the battery, but also the utilized cell chemistry and design. For this reason, precise knowledge of the deployed battery cell must be acquired to determine the optimal break-even point regarding both charge duration and aging. This can also be achieved through the use of battery models, allowing various combinations of operating conditions to be examined with less laboratory effort.

To derive optimized fast-charging operation strategies, knowledge of the utilized cell chemistry and design and battery and ambient conditions must be leveraged. The main objective of this work is to demonstrate the influence of these factors on the charge duration for two different example cells using a model-based anode potential control strategy to prevent the onset of lithium plating. The resulting observations are compared with conventional preheating methodologies to estimate the time-saving potential when utilized for fast-charge events. The main contributions of this work therefore include the following:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Overview of the state of the art of battery preconditioning in the literature and recent commercially available electric vehicles An overview of currently examined lithium-ion battery preheating techniques is provided and compared with the utilized topologies as known from currently available electric vehicles.

Analysis of the influence of ambient conditions on fast-charging in the Tesla Model 3 Using vehicle data logged from a Tesla Model 3, charging durations under different ambient conditions as well as state-of-the-art preconditioning strategies for fast-charge purposes are demonstrated.

Examination of the influence of cell type on fast-charging and self-heating behavior Two commercially available 18650 lithium-ion battery cells are compared regarding electrical and thermal behavior when charged at increasing C-rates. For this purpose, laboratory measurements and simulations are performed for both cells, demonstrating pronounced differences in behavior despite comparable datasheet characteristics.

Quantification of the influence of initial temperature, starting SoC and age on fast-charging capability The influence of initial conditions on fast-charge events is examined using electrochemical battery models. The regarded cells are simulated under an anode potential control strategy to prevent lithium plating, thus representing the fastest possible charging protocol without lithium deposition.

Analysis of the time-saving potential through active heating versus self-heating of the lithium-ion cells Selected heating techniques reported from the literature are compared to the inherent self-heating of the batteries to estimate achievable time-saving potential through active battery heating.

ce:section.ce:para: The remainder of the article is organized as follows: Section 2 examines previously reported heating techniques from past literature as well as deployed preconditioning strategies in commercially available electric vehicles by example of recorded bus data from a Tesla Model 3. Following this, Section 3 presents the utilized modeling methodologies for both cells as well as a comparison of the electro-thermal behavior for both batteries. Section 4 presents the results of the simulation study and examines the possible time-saving potential for different heating strategies. Finally, Section 5 summarizes the potential of model-based fast-charging for the derivation of optimized vehicle operation strategies and examines future avenues of work.

For battery preconditioning to reap the desired benefits regarding achievable charging speed and degradation, preheating techniques that allow the temperature to be increased in a short amount of time need to be implemented. For this purpose, several methodologies have been proposed in past literature and summarized in various review papers [8–12]. These can be divided into external and internal heating methods, depending on whether an external heating medium is applied to the cell or internal heat generation such as through thermal losses is utilized [8]. An overview of the different techniques reported in the literature can be seen in Table 1. While the reported literature also contains heating rate values, it should be noted that these differ depending on different factors such as battery size, which could lead to different values in real applications.

Current electric vehicles mainly rely on air or liquid-based thermal management systems [26]. While air-based systems offer several benefits such as low cost, complexity, maintenance requirements and weight, they suffer from poor thermal conductivity and heat capacity, which is why they have been deployed less in recent vehicles, mainly still being utilized by some Japanese and Chinese car manufacturers [27]. These disadvantages also limit the potential benefits of air-based preheating technology for fast-charging, as they translate to long heating times with high energy consumption and pack temperature distribution [8,11].

To improve upon these disadvantages, liquid-based thermal management systems are frequently used in current electric vehicles and can be considered state of the art, with examples ranging from manufacturers such as Tesla and General Motors [27] as well as BMW [28] to Mercedes [29], Porsche [30], Audi [31] or Lucid Motors, from which we examined Lucid Air battery modules. In a recent publication, a tear-down analysis was performed for a Volkswagen ID.3, demonstrating the performance of the installed liquid thermal management system [32]. While many other examples exist, these are not listed for brevity. In the context of battery preconditioning, a commonly used topology consists of a heater and a heat exchanger as well as a pump and circulation pipes [8], for which heating rates of up to 5K/min can be achieved [13–15]. In current vehicles, for example, the Volkswagen ID.3, two electric heaters are installed. The passenger cabin is heated by an air-side positive temperature coefficient (PTC) heater, whereas the battery is heated by a coolant heater [32]. An alternative approach is taken by Tesla, which uses the electric motor to heat up the battery in the Tesla Model 3 and Model Y [33]. If insufficient waste heat is generated, additional heat can be generated in so-called waste-heat mode, during which the power electronics provide additional power to the motor stator without generating rotation [33]. However, these approaches can be disadvantageous in terms of energy efficiency compared with an electric heater, since significant heat losses to the environment must be expected and the thermal masses of the electric motor have to be heated up.

Several alternative thermal management concepts have been proposed in the literature, such as phase-change materials (PCMs) [8,34], electrothermal elements [8–10] or internal heating methods through the application of current to the battery [10]. External resistive heating has, for example, been implemented in the Nissan Leaf [28] or the BMW i3, which utilizes heating wires on the battery. Heating speeds of up to 60K/min have been achieved using resistive heating with foils inserted into the battery cell itself [35], which has however only been demonstrated in laboratory environments [8]. While these methods offer promising results regarding achievable heating speeds, they are yet to be deployed within state-of-the-art electric vehicles.

Within automotive applications, preconditioning strategies using the technologies above can range from maintaining adequate minimum operation temperatures to targeted preheating strategies with the purpose of achieving more favorable conditions before fast-charging events. The impact of initial conditions and battery preconditioning strategies in state-of-the-art electric vehicles can be demonstrated in the following by examining logged bus data from a Tesla Model 3 [36]. Fig. 1 shows the charge durations for three fast-charge events at a 250kW charging station from different starting SoCs to 80% SoC at a lower and higher battery temperature of on average 17°C and 38°C, respectively.

The cases with the warm battery show a charging duration of 27min decreasing almost linearly to 13min at a medium SoC. The cold battery, however, requires a charging time of 52min, only decreasing to 38min for a medium SoC with diminishing time benefits towards higher SoCs contrary to the warm battery. This translates to an almost twofold charging duration increase at a starting SoC of 10%, nearly tripling towards medium SoCs. This demonstrates not only the adverse impact of cold temperatures on the fast-charging capability of electric vehicles under realistic operating conditions but also the dependence on the starting SoC. To counteract these effects, Tesla utilizes an active preheating strategy, during which loss heat is generated within the electric motor and fed into the battery thermal management system. Two such battery heating processes can be observed in Fig. 2, which shows a preconditioned case, during which the battery is heated in advance en route by selecting a Tesla supercharger as the navigation target, as well as an unconditioned case, in which the cold battery is instantaneously connected to the charging station without prior active heating. In the preconditioned case, the battery coolant temperature increases around an hour before the fast-charge event, warming the battery to 40°C. This battery temperature is maintained and allowed to increase to below 50°C during the charging process. In the unconditioned case without prior heating, the battery is still actively heated parallel to the charging event, accelerating the temperature increase until it reaches almost 40°C towards the end. By thus maintaining higher battery temperatures, faster charging times can be achieved.

While battery preheating can be beneficial in allowing shorter fast-charging times while limiting degradation, it also has disadvantages that must be taken into account. Higher temperatures reduce the risk of lithium plating while accelerating adverse side reactions, which could increase other degradation mechanisms [6]. Battery preheating is also associated with additional energy consumption, which is a cost factor, and negatively impacts vehicle range and energy efficiency. In the case of the Tesla Model 3 strategy, logged drivetrain signals reveal that the motor draws between 2.5kW and 3kW of power in waste heat mode to heat the battery at the charging station [36], which corresponds to an energy consumption of up to 2kWh for a 40min charge, or almost 3.7% of the installed battery capacity of 54.6kWh of the vehicle. However, previous literature has argued that fast charging can be performed depending on the initial battery state while allowing the battery to heat only from the charging process under adiabatic conditions, which would render active preconditioning obsolete [7]. This has led recent research to propose a joint optimization of charging current with active preheating [37]. A recent study used an electrochemical battery model to examine the influence of different thermal management strategies on fast-charged large-format full-tab cylindrical cells, demonstrating the potential of model-based cooling and heating strategies as opposed to simple rule-based preconditioning [38]. However, the influence of different starting SoCs and aging states, which are to be expected in automotive applications, was not examined. As these effects depend on the utilized cell and ambient conditions, precise knowledge of both the battery and the achievable fast-charging behavior depending on the conditions before the charging event has to be leveraged. This could allow battery preheating to only be performed when necessary, achieving a trade-off between fast-charging acceleration, energy consumption, and side-reaction acceleration due to elevated temperatures.

Two example cells with respective models are utilized to examine the influence of ambient conditions on the model-based fast-charging behavior of lithium-ion batteries. The cells are compared through constant current fast-charge tests in the laboratory before a model-based fast-charging method is applied to each cell model to investigate the expected behavior under differing starting conditions.

For the purpose of this article, two high-power 18650 cells are examined, namely the Sony/Murata VTC5A with a nickel cobalt aluminium oxide Li(NixCoyAlz)O2 (NCA) cathode and silicon-doped graphite anode [32] as well as the TerraE INR18650-25P, which uses a nickel cobalt manganese oxide Li(NixCoyMnz)O2 (NMC) cathode and regular graphite anode. These cells are chosen because they offer similar capacities and voltage bounds while differing in cell chemistry. An overview of the data sheet specifications for both cells can be seen in Table 2. The Sony/Murata VTC5A was examined in depth in a previous study [39].

Tests under fast-charge cycling conditions at different charge rates are performed to examine the differences in the electrical and thermal behavior of the utilized cells. For this purpose, the battery cells are placed inside a Binder KB115 climate chamber at an ambient temperature of 0°C and connected to a Basytec XCTS battery cycler. This low temperature was chosen to ensure conditions to provoke negative anode potentials and lithium plating, which can later be examined using the battery models to evaluate the effect of the cell self-heating behavior. Similarly to a previous study, the cells are initially fully discharged to the minimum cut-off voltage of 2.5V with a cut-off current of 50mA or 1/50 C [40]. The cells are then charged with a constant current of 1C up to 6C until the upper voltage limit of 4.2V is reached while monitoring the cell surface temperature in the middle of the cell cans using Pt100 Sensors attached with thermal compound [40]. The resulting voltage and temperature profiles can be seen in Fig. 3, with the Sony/Murata VTC5A on the left and the TerraE INR18650-25P on the right. Both cells can be observed to show similar behaviors to each other, with the VTC5A, however, displaying slightly lower overvoltages and a stronger temperature rise during the fast-charge, reaching 5°C higher temperatures than the TerraE INR18650-25P. This matches observations made in previous literature, which argues that NCA batteries tend to demonstrate higher temperature rises, while batteries based on NMC chemistry offer better thermal behavior [41]. The difference in overpotentials may indicate a higher sensitivity of the TerraE INR18650-25P to lower temperature conditions, which could also indicate a higher sensitivity towards lithium plating. This could be why the TerraE cell shows lower current ratings than the VTC5A despite otherwise possessing similar datasheet specifications. It should be noted here that these values only represent the temperatures measured at the cell can and only allow limited conclusions to be drawn about the cell core temperature. Thermal gradients within the battery cell, which are especially pronounced in large format cells as commonly used in automotive applications, can lead to localized increases in lithium plating, which is why spatially resolved knowledge of the thermal behavior may be required for more accurate predictions to be made [38]. A teardown analysis of both cells is provided in the Appendix.

To examine the effect of different initial and ambient conditions on the fast-charging behavior of both cells, previously implemented battery models are utilized. For the Sony/Murata VTC5A, an electro-thermal single particle model (SPM) as proposed by Moura et al. [42] and parameterized in a previous study is deployed [40], while the TerraE INR18650-25P cell is examined using a commercially available cell model by the company Batemo [43]. The SPM under study was extensively validated under different temperature conditions and C-rates, as well as successfully used to implement an anode-potential control aging study in two previous publications, to which we refer to for brevity [40,44]. Both models are implemented in Matlab/Simulink. They can predict internal battery states, including the anode potential, which can be leveraged to implement fast-charging strategies based on anode potential control to prevent lithium plating. This concept can be seen in Fig. 4, which shows a simulated charging process for the VTC5A cell from 0% to 80% SoC at an ambient temperature of -10°C. The charging current, which is initially set to a charge rate of 6C, is controlled and derated using a PID controller to maintain anode potentials above a safety threshold of 50mV while remaining below the defined maximum current for the respective charge. While lithium plating can be prevented by maintaining positive anode potentials [45], this reserve is required to account for model and measurement inaccuracies [40]. As the anode potential reaches the defined threshold, the charging current is derated until the target SoC of 80% is reached. Towards the end of the charging process, the charging current can be observed to increase again due to the battery warming up, which leads to reduced overpotentials and improved cell kinetics. To prevent unsafe or unrealistic battery operating conditions, further control loops are implemented to derate the charging current to maintain the datasheet voltage limit of 4.2V as well as to implement a simplified cooling strategy that applies a cooling power of 4W to the cell and derates the current to maintain temperatures below 60°C. This cooling power corresponds to a heat transfer coefficient of 800W/(m2K), which has been cited as a realistic value for cooling plates in automotive batteries in previous literature [38]. Heat losses to ambient are taken into account using a heat transfer coefficient of 10W/(m2K), which is a typical value for 18650 cells with natural convection [46]. Although some studies have indicated that datasheet voltage limits can be safely exceeded when plating overpotential limits are used instead [47], the more conservative datasheet specifications are utilized to reflect state-of-the-art usage. Prototypes of model-based fast-charging strategies should deploy more sophisticated controllers to consider safety and thermal limits. However, this would exceed the scope of this paper, which focuses on assessing the influence of battery condition on achievable anode potential control strategy charge durations.

By modeling the internal parameters of a lithium-ion cell, fast-charging strategies can be devised that operate along the physical boundaries of the battery, allowing increased charging speeds with simultaneously reduced aging. However, as the cells degrade, the initial parameters change, leading to changed behavior and charging constraints that vary over time. To take this into account, fast-charging strategies require derating mechanisms, which consider aging to adjust the charging protocols over time [44]. To examine the potential effects of such derating strategies in the context of initial condition sensitivity and preheating methodologies, we additionally adjust the utilized electrochemical battery models to account for a hypothetical increase in resistance and a reduction of usable capacity, as described and demonstrated in a previous publication [44]. This is achieved by scaling the initial cell capacity according to the remaining full cell capacity
SOH
C
as follows [44]:

For this purpose, we assume homogeneous degradation of the anode and cathode [44]. Resistance increase is modeled by scaling the SEI resistance according to the full cell resistance increase
SOH
R
[44]:

=
R
SEI,initial
⋅
SOH
R

For this purpose, example degradation values were taken from measurements on a commercial 18650 NCA cell in the literature [48]. The cell was cycled using a real-world electric vehicle driving cycle, leading to a resistance increase of 12% at 90% remaining capacity and 38% at 80% remaining capacity [48]. While these values depend on the specific cell used and the operating conditions under which the cells are cycled, other studies in the literature have achieved comparable results, which is why the values can be used to make a qualitative assessment of the fast-charge performance of aged cells [49–51].

Using the simulated anode potential control fast-charging methodology, the influence of initial cell temperature and starting SoC as well as state-of-health (SoH) on fast-charge capability in regard to the resulting charging time is examined in the following sections. Section 4.1 examines the increases in charging duration due to adverse initial conditions, demonstrating the potential of charge scheduling and preconditioning prior to the charging event. Section 4.2 examines the time-saving potential achievable with different heating strategies while the battery is charging without prior preconditioning.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of initial conditions on charging duration
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 shows the resulting charging times at different initial SoCs and temperatures for the Sony/Murata VTC5A in the left column and the TerraE INR18650-25P in the right column. The charging processes are simulated at maximum charging rates of 1C up to 4C in each row, which represents the initial C-rate before derating is required to maintain anode potentials above the defined reserve.

At low charging rates, the charging duration remains mostly temperature-independent when charging the VTC5A. Increasing charging rates, however, lead to a more than two-fold increase in charge duration at subzero temperatures and SoC ranges between 30% and 60%, compared to lower starting SoCs. Thermal causes can explain this, as the battery does not have enough time to heat up through the fast-charging process before the current is derated, which in turn leads to an overall extension of the charge duration for batteries charged from medium SoCs compared to cases in which the full charging current can be applied for a longer period of time.

The TerraE INR18650-25P displays an increased sensitivity to both starting SoC and temperature over all simulated charging rates, with charging processes from medium starting SoCs showing significantly increased durations even at moderate temperatures of up to 20°C. This effect is more pronounced at higher C-rates; however, contrary to the VTC5A, the 25P shows increased cold temperature charging times over a wider SoC range between 10% and 60% SoC. This leads to the cell showing almost no decrease in charging time with increased starting C-rates unless the starting temperature lies in the range of 20°C and above. This demonstrates a higher sensitivity of the anode potential to varying ambient conditions for the 25P, leading to an increased susceptibility to lithium plating during regular operation and especially fast-charge events.

This is reflected in Fig. 6, which shows example cross-sections of the charging time increases for both cells at an initial temperature of -10°C and SoC of 40% relative to the respective fastest charging time under these conditions. The charging time increases are displayed for hypothetical battery SoHs of 100%, 90%, and 80%. As can be seen in Fig. 6(a), the VTC5A and 25P display an over twofold and over fourfold charging duration increase at medium SoCs respectively. At a starting SoC of 40% in Fig. 6(b), the charge duration increases threefold for the VTC5A and more than sixfold for the TerraE 25P when charged at -10°C compared to 40°C. This emphasizes the previously observed higher sensitivity of the TerraE 25P to colder temperatures when fast charging. These observations can be explained, among other, through the self-heating behavior of both cells. Compared to the TerraE 25P, the VTC5A cell heats up faster during fast charging and reaches higher maximum temperatures, which is beneficial for the deployed lithium plating prevention strategy, as higher temperatures result in reduced risk of negative anode potentials.

As the batteries age using the previously introduced degradation scenarios, increased charge durations and sensitivity to initial conditions can be observed. Although the battery capacity reduces over time, the increased resistance through aging mechanisms such as SEI growth leads to higher overpotentials, reducing the anode potential and increasing the likelihood of lithium plating. This leads to the charging current being reduced earlier, reducing the self-heating potential and prolonging the charge duration over a wider range of starting conditions.

This effect grows as the battery ages, emphasizing the need for fast-charge applications to take the degradation state of the battery into account when implementing fast-charge events. Besides an increased dependency of charge duration on starting SoC, the initial temperature also shows a growing negative impact on achievable charging speeds. This is especially noticeable with the TerraE 25P, for which a more pronounced impact of starting temperature can be observed, leading to a tenfold increase in charging duration relative to 40°C at a starting temperature of -10°C when an SoH of 80% is reached for medium SoCs. While the cells examined show increasing charging times as they age, the influence this has is expected to differ strongly between individual batteries. This is due to opposite influencing effects on the anode potential throughout the battery lifetime. While increasing overpotentials promote the risk of lithium plating, they also lead to higher ohmic losses, which increases the heat generation in the cell and can thus aid in beneficial self-heating depending on the utilized cell. Thus, future fast-charging and thermal management operation strategies should take the individual aging trajectories of the utilized cells into account besides temperature and SoC to achieve optimal performance and efficiency throughout the expected lifetime.

To counteract the observed adverse effects of unsuitable initial battery conditions when fast-charging, preheating strategies have been proposed and are increasingly implemented in state-of-the-art electric vehicles. While preconditioning strategies are capable of heating the battery en route to the charging station, an alternative approach involves heating the battery during the charging process while the vehicle is connected to the charger. This avoids a reduction in range, analogously to the method deployed in the Tesla Model 3 in the case of no en route heating. By applying external heating power within the models, the time-saving potential of thermal conditioning during the charge sequence can be estimated.

For this purpose, heating powers of 0.33W, 1W, and 15W are examined per cell, representing both the lower and higher spectrum of heating rates reported from the literature as well as a hypothetical intermediate case [8–12]. For the purpose of this examination, starting SoCs of 0% and 40% are chosen. The simulations are performed for a new cell as well as for the aging scenario at 80% SoH. The results of these simulations can be seen in Fig. 7 for the TerraE 25P and Fig. 8 for the Sony/Murata VTC5A, which show the 4C charging times using the examined heating rates if the batteries are heated from different starting temperatures to increasing target temperatures up to 40°C. This is simulated for starting SoCs of 0%, and 40% in the respective rows and the three examined heating powers in the columns.

As can be seen in Figs. 7 and 8, the time-saving potential for heating while charging depends strongly on the initial and target temperature as well as the available heating speed. Choosing higher heating target temperatures and heating powers leads to significantly increased charging speeds due to improved reaction kinetics leading to higher anode potentials, which delay the necessary derating of the charging current. This allows all charging processes to be completed in under 30min, regardless of the simulated initial conditions. This effect is more pronounced for the TerraE 25P due to the cell’s previously observed higher sensitivity of the anode potential control strategy to lower temperatures as well as the more pronounced self-heating of the VTC5A, which renders active heating more redundant.

As initial temperatures increase, heating offers sinking benefits, with the VTC5A showing almost no time-saving potential for heating powers of 1W and below. For sufficiently high heating speeds, however, both cells benefit even at a comparatively high starting temperature of 10°C, allowing the charge duration to be shortened by up to 50%. Heating to higher target temperatures shows diminishing returns for both cells unless high heating speeds can be achieved. This is because the cells are then able to reach higher temperatures before the current is derated, and the SoC limit is reached compared with slower heating. For both cells, heating offers the most time-saving potential at medium SoCs, where anode potential control otherwise quickly leads to current derating. However, this is strongly dependent on the utilized cell. While the VTC5A displays almost no benefit from heating at low starting SoCs due to the lower sensitivity of the anode potential and stronger self-heating, the 25P charge duration can potentially be reduced by around 70% for a new cell. This confirms predictions made by Pegel et al. [38], showing notable differences in optimal thermal management strategies for fast charging depending on the utilized cell. Pegel et al. observed pronounced current derating due to thermal limits at high heating powers and starting temperatures, requiring cooling and leading to heating mostly being beneficial at subzero temperatures and heating powers above 2W showing diminishing benefits [38]. Contrary to this, the cells examined in this study almost always benefit from faster heating with higher target temperatures, as the assumed cooling is sufficient to maintain thermal limits without derating the current. This may point to a difference between small and large-format battery cells, with the latter running a higher risk of overheating due to the more pronounced thermal gradients within the cell and higher thermal capacities. Besides cell format, tab design has also been identified as a significant influence factor on the fast-charging capability of batteries and the choice of cooling system [52]. This and the adverse effect high temperatures may have on other degradation mechanisms should be examined in future research. It should be noted that while this study examines cells under free convection, vehicle battery packs are thermally more isolated and introduce the issue of thermal gradients, which is expected to impact the resulting suitable thermal management strategy.

Besides the initial conditions, the SoH of both batteries negatively impacts the fast-charging capability, leading to increased charge durations over almost all examined cases. However, an exception can be seen in some simulated cases where the aged battery can be charged slightly faster than the new case. This could be due to the previously discussed opposite effects on the anode potential, leading to aged batteries showing increased beneficial self-heating. With sufficiently high heating, however, the negative impact of battery degradation can be almost entirely eliminated, highlighting the importance of battery heating to maintain guaranteed charging speeds throughout the full lifetime of an electric vehicle under a diverse range of operating conditions. These conclusions are however drawn based on the assumption of homogeneous electrode degradation. We expect inhomogeneous degradation of anode, cathode, and lithium inventory depending on the use case, which should be more closely examined in future research.

Heating thresholds should thus be adjusted according to the initial conditions of the cell, as the additional energy expenditure does not always lead to significant benefits, and higher temperatures can also have adverse effects on battery lifetime.

In this study, we utilized electrochemical battery models with an anode potential control strategy for lithium plating prevention to examine the influence of ambient and cell conditions on the fast-charging capability of different lithium-ion batteries. For low temperatures and medium states of charge, we show that charging times can be increased by up to 900% in the worst case, depending on the utilized cell. This is reflected in state-of-the-art electric vehicles, with the Tesla Model 3 showing an increase in the fast-charge duration of 192% under unsuitable initial conditions without prior conditioning. These effects further increase as the batteries degrade, leading to diminishing fast-charging capability, which can be countered by heating before the charging event. If this is not possible due to a lack of time or prior knowledge, significant time-saving potential can be achieved for battery heating while charging, allowing charge durations to be decreased by up to 80%. This allows operation over a wide range of conditions and almost fully eliminates the adverse effects of battery aging on fast-charge capability with sufficiently high installed heating power. While current state-of-the-art electric vehicles such as the Tesla Model 3 already implement heating and preconditioning strategies, this study shows that the time-saving potential and thus the optimal heating strategy differs depending on the utilized cell as well as battery state of charge, age, starting and target temperature, charging rate, and available heating speed. Especially the self-heating behavior of fast-charged batteries of different chemistries is shown to influence the achievable charging speeds, leading to significant differences in achievable charge durations despite similar battery datasheet specifications and cell designs.
The main conclusions for future fast-charging strategies can thus be summarized as follows:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Active heating allows the influence of adverse ambient and initial battery conditions to be minimized By heating the battery before and during fast-charging, charge durations can be reduced under unsuitable initial conditions. Especially en route, battery preconditioning promises to eliminate adverse SoC and temperature dependencies of the fast-charging capability of batteries. This would allow suitable temperatures to be reached even with lower installed heating powers, but it requires precise planning and sufficient time prior to the charging event to achieve the maximum benefit without consuming more energy than required or maintaining elevated battery temperatures for longer than necessary.

Fast-charging strategy development requires cell-individual optimization The utilized battery cell design and chemistry, available thermal management system, vehicle operation scenario, and ambient conditions result in vastly different fast-charging behaviors. Fast charging is thus a highly complex and cell-individual problem that requires precise optimization catered towards the concrete use case.

Fast-charging optimization requires a suitable model-based approach To achieve optimal fast-charging strategies under diverse operating conditions, novel model-based optimization methodologies are required. These would allow the prediction of suitable charging and thermal management strategies but depend on the development of methodologies with feasible implementation effort and suitability for utilization in vehicles.

ce:section.ce:para: While rapid battery heating can be beneficial in certain edge use cases, it adds complexity, cost, and weight. The highest advantage can be achieved by intelligently heating the battery before the charging event, leveraging charge scheduling and knowledge of the self-heating behavior of the battery. This can be achieved even with current commercially used heating technologies with heating rates in the range of 1K/min. It is therefore expected that, depending on the initial conditions and utilized cells, as well as optimization goals, there exists an optimal fast-charge and thermal management strategy for increased charging speed and reduced degradation and energy consumption, which shall be further examined in the context of vehicle integration in future research.

Kareem Abo Gamra: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Nikolaos Wassiliadis: Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Christian Allgäuer: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Markus Lienkamp: Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the funding provided by the German Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Energy(BMWi) within the project “ultraBatt” under grant number 01MV21015D. The authors would like to thank Batemo for providing battery models and teardown data for the TerraE INR18650-25P cell, as well as the BMZ Group for providing the cells. The authors also thank Thomas Schöpfel for his support of this study during his master’s studies.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix
ce:section.ce:para: The geometrical properties of the two examined cells can be seen in Figs. A.9 and A.10. The cells show similar design properties, with two anode tabs placed on opposite ends of the electrode on uncoated copper foil. This reduces electrical resistance and aids heat transfer from the cell core to the cell can, which is why many lithium-ion batteries utilize this design [39,53]. Both cells also show a large area of uncoated copper on the outer side of the anode, which wraps around the remaining opposite electrode without matching active material on the cathode [39]. While the anodes show similar geometrical dimensions, the TerraE 25P possesses a lower fraction of coated material, instead showing larger patches of uncoated copper on both ends of the front and back of the electrode. The cathodes are equally coated with active material on both sides and have a single aluminum tab placed in the middle of the electrodes with an offset towards the cell core. While a symmetrical tab placement would yield the lowest electrical resistance, offsets towards the cell core are deployed to achieve improved thermal behavior, as this prevents the current collector from being positioned too close to the cell can during winding and thus increases the heat flow out of the cell [39,53]. Despite the similar dimensions of the electrodes, the TerraE 25P cathode current collector is offset around 10cm further towards the cell core and possesses a slightly larger zone of uncoated aluminum. An oversizing of the anode compared to the cathode is observed for both cells, which is a common design feature to ensure available anode active material, preventing local lithium plating, which could occur due to electrode misalignments during production [39,54].
